coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-based carbon dots as highly biocompatible and sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of vitamin P in fruit samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chenzhao
Wang, Xiujuan
Zhang, Yupu
Tang, Yihuan
Yang, Yi
Wang, Bin
Wei, Shanshan
Wang, Zhibing
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Vitamin P (VP) known as rutin is one of the common flavonoids, which widely exists in fruits and vegetables and often used as a dietary additive. The rapid and accurate detection of VP in food matrices is critical for evaluating food quality and guiding diet. Herein, a rapid, accurate, and selective detection scheme for VP in fruit samples was proposed for the first time using ionic liquid-based carbon dots (IL-CDs). The synthesized IL-CDs exhibited great biocompatibility and excellent optical properties including high fluorescence intensity, high quantum yield, and good fluorescence stability. Through an internal filtering effect (IFE), VP could greatly reduce the fluorescence of these CDs. In the present study, this probe demonstrated good sensitivity and excellent selectivity toward VP with a low detection limit of 60.0 nmol/L. Moreover, this approach was effectively applied to detect VP in food samples with a recovery range of 97 % to 119 %. More interestingly, the results of cell imaging suggested that IL-CDs were expected to be promising material for bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Ionic liquid
Vitamin P
Food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM and HR-TEM image, (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of the IL-CDs.

(A) VP concentrations (0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0 μmol/L) on the fluorescence intensity. Inset is the relationship between fluorescence intensity and VP and (B) Effect of different interfering substances.

(A) UV − vis absorption and (B) fluorescence spectra of IL-CDs, VP, and VP/IL-CDs. (C) Lifetimes of the IL-CDs in the absence and presence of VP. (D) UV–vis absorption of VP and the excitation and emission of the IL-CDs.
Synthesis and application of the IL-CDs.
Recovery of VP in Food Samples.

Inital VP amount determined by HPLC
(μmol/L)
Inital VP amount determined by IL-CDs (μmol/L)

B. Wang, C., Wang, Q., Zhong, M., & Kan, X. (2016). Boronic acid based imprinted electrochemical sensor for rutin recognition and detection. Analyst, 141(20), 5792–5798.

C. Song, X.-Y., Meng, X., Xiao, B.-L., Li, Y.-Y., Ma, X.-X., Moosavi-Movahedi, A. A., & Hong, J. (2022). MWCNTs-CTAB and HFs-Lac nanocomposite-modified glassy carbon electrode for rutin determination. Biosensors, 2022, 12, 632.

D. Brugnerotto, P., Silva, T. R., Brondani, D., Zapp, E., & Vieira, I. C. (2017). Gold nanoparticles stabilized in β-Cyclodextrin and decorated with laccase applied in the construction of a biosensor for rutin. Electroanalysis, 2017, 29, 1031.

E. Sinduja, B., & Abraham John, S. (2018). Sensitive determination of rutin by spectrofluorimetry using carbon dots synthesized from a non-essential amino acid. Spectrochim Acta A Mol Biomol Spectrosc, 193, 486–491.

F. Wang, C., Shang, S., Zheng, X., Lei, P., Han, J., Yuan, L., Li, Z., Wang, R., Gong, W., Tang, J., & Yang, Y. (2018). Fluorescent sensors based on Cu-doped carbon quantum dots for the detection of rutin. Journal of the Brazilian Chemical Society.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-based carbon dots as highly biocompatible and sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of vitamin P in fruit samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin State Tobacco Monopoly Administration, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin State Tobacco Monopoly Administration, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, Yan’an Street 2055, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vitamin P (VP) known as rutin is one of the common flavonoids, which widely exists in fruits and vegetables and often used as a dietary additive. The rapid and accurate detection of VP in food matrices is critical for evaluating food quality and guiding diet. Herein, a rapid, accurate, and selective detection scheme for VP in fruit samples was proposed for the first time using ionic liquid-based carbon dots (IL-CDs). The synthesized IL-CDs exhibited great biocompatibility and excellent optical properties including high fluorescence intensity, high quantum yield, and good fluorescence stability. Through an internal filtering effect (IFE), VP could greatly reduce the fluorescence of these CDs. In the present study, this probe demonstrated good sensitivity and excellent selectivity toward VP with a low detection limit of 60.0 nmol/L. Moreover, this approach was effectively applied to detect VP in food samples with a recovery range of 97 % to 119 %. More interestingly, the results of cell imaging suggested that IL-CDs were expected to be promising material for bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin P (VP), known as rutin, is one of the most common and bioactive natural flavonoids. It is widely found in some fruits and vegetables, such as lemons, grapes, and eggplant (Lue, Sorensen, Jacobsen, Guo, & Xu, 2017). VP not only plays a critical role in lowering blood pressure and expanding capillaries but also has good effects on anti-inflammation and anti-cancer (Sun, Wang, Gong, Cheng, Shi, & Sun, 2013). Therefore, it has been widely applied in food and pharmaceutical products. However, the excessive or long-term intake of VP may stimulate gastrointestinal mucosa and cause physical symptoms such as sickness, puking, lack of appetite, stomach pain, and diarrhoea. As a result, developing a simple, rapid, and sensitive scheme for determining VP in food to evaluate food quality and guide diet is critical.

At present, chemiluminescence (CL) (Liu, Yu, & MacGregor, 2007), electrochemistry (Ensafi & Hajian, 2006), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Zhang, Qi, Zou, & Li, 2013), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Dmitrienko, Stepanova, Kudrinskaya, & Apyari, 2013) are widely applied to the detection of VP. However, these methods have a large number of disadvantages, such as complicated operation, the requirement for expensive equipment, and excessive consumption of toxic organic solvents. In recent years, fluorometry has been employed in the detection of VP because of its high sensitivity and simple operation (Cai et al., 2021). Moreover, fluorescent materials are critical in the application of fluorescence technologies. However, most organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots have biological toxicity and poor optical stability, which limits their further application (Tang et al., 2017; Xin, Li, Wu, Zhao, & Song, 2017).

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been favored as a new type of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials due to low cost, great optical characteristics, and negligible cytotoxicity (Chandra, Das, Bag, Laha, & Pramanik, 2011; Wang et al., 2018b; Yu, Li, Zeng, Zheng, & Wu, 2013). These characteristics make them widely applied in sensing, photocatalysis, photoelectric devices, biomedicine, etc (Al-Hashimi, Omer, & Rahman, 2020; Han et al., 2018; Yuan, Li, Fan, Meng, Fan, & Yang, 2016; Zhang, Wang, Cui, Zhou, & Shen, 2018). Particularly, their good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity distinguish them from traditional organic dyes. In the last few years, some compounds such as asparagine and Na2[Cu(EDTA)] were selected as precursors to synthesize CDs for the determining of VP in tablets (Sinduja & Abraham John, 2018; Wang et al., 2018a). Although the synthesis process of the above CDs was simple, these CDs were not applied to complex systems such as food and biology. Hence, the development of selective, sensitive, outstanding optical, and biocompatible CDs for sensing VP in complex systems is still highly desired. Since the properties of CDs depend heavily on the precursor, it is of great significance to select a suitable precursor. Recently, ionic liquids (ILs) have been considered as ideal precursors for synthesizing CDs because most ILs contain large conjugated structures and are doped with nitrogen, boron, fluorine, and other elements that can enhance the optical properties of CDs (Zheng, Guo, Guan, Chen, Shu, & Wang, 2019). New red-emission CDs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal strategy using phenylenediamine and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (Zhang et al., 2020). Sulfur-nitrogen doped CDs were synthesized using ILs for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution (Zhuo et al., 2017). These CDs exhibited excellent optical and sensing properties, indicating that ILs had great potential in the synthesis of CDs. Since most of the detection methods are unable to selectively detect a single target compound in a complex matrix, and the detection mechanism is not discussed in depth. In consideration of the advantages of ionic liquid-based carbon dots (IL-CDs), we attempted to design some biocompatible IL-CDs with specificity for VP to meet the urgent need for rapid and accurate detection of VP in complex matrices.

Herein, a new IL ([OMIM]PF4) was selected for the first time to synthesize IL-CDs by the microwave irradiation method for the rapid, accurate and selective detection of VP in fruit samples (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized IL-CDs have excellent optical properties and great biocompatibility and demonstrated high sensitivity and excellent selectivity toward VP, and the detection limit is much lower than other VP assays (60.0 nmol/L). The UV–vis absorption spectra, FT-IR spectra, Parker's equation, and fluorescence lifetime were thoroughly investigated, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was found to be internal filtration effect (IFE). Moreover, the practical applications of this fluorescent sensing approach in the determination of VP in food samples (tangerine, orange, lemon, grapefruit, and grape) were successfully realized. Additionally, the synthesized IL-CDs exhibited great potential in the field of cell imaging. Therefore, these IL-CDs could be dedicated to more applications in the sensing and bioimaging fields.

1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4), 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([HMIM]PF4) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([BMIM]PF4) were purchased from Chengjie Chemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin P (VP), kaempferol (Kae), quercetin (Que), tiliroside (Til), delphinidin-3-O-glucoside chloride (Del), cyanidin-3-O-glucoside chloride (Cya), diuron (Diu), monuron (Mon), vitamin C (VC), and vitamin E (VE) were provided by Shanghaiyuanye Bio-Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tangerine, orange, lemon, grapefruit, and grapes were purchased from a market (Changchun, China). The Milli-Q water purification system provided the deionized water utilized in the experiments.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM) was utilized to investigate the morphology of IL-CDs (JEOL, JEM-2100F). FT-IR spectra and Raman spectra were performed on an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermofisher, Nicolet IS50) and a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, LabRAM HR Evolution). A zeta potential meter (PSS, Nicomp 380) was used to measure zeta potential. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded from 10° to 80° on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, SmartLab-9 kW). The element content and valence were collected on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Malvern Panalytical, X'Pert3 MRD). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained on a UV–vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary50) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC), respectively. A fluorescent microscope was used to obtain cell imaging (Leica, DMI4000B).

Initially, 2 g of [OMIM]BF4 was put in a 50 mL Teflon beaker and warmed for 80 s in a household microwave oven (490 W, Galanz, China). The obtained liquid was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol. Then, the solution was immersed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for 24 h to remove small molecules. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was filtered through a membrane with a 0.22 μm pore size. The resulting solution was freeze-dried for further characterization.

Initially, 200 μL of IL-CDs solution was added into the VP solution (2.8 mL) and then shaken for 3 min. The emission spectra were then obtained using a 360 nm excitation wavelength. Finally, the selectivity was examined by mixing them with solutions containing various interfering compounds (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, F−, Cys, His, Glu, Arg, Diu, Mon, Kae, Que, Til, Del, Cya, VC, VE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of VP in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The external standard calibration method was carried out to assess the feasibility of the current experiment. Briefly, five fruits with different VP contents (tangerine, orange, lemon, grapefruit, and grape) were selected as representative samples for analysis performance evaluation. Firstly, the edible part of the fruit was accurately weighed (50 g), squeezed into juice, and placed into 100 mL of ethanol for 30 min. After centrifuging the sample solution at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size membrane for further analysis. The obtained sample solution was diluted 10 times, and the fluorescence signal was detected at a 360 nm excitation wavelength with the same detection method of VP. The VP in the sample was quantified according to the standard curve of 3.5.1.

The cytotoxicity assay of the IL-CDs toward MCF-7 and Hela cells was measured by the MTT assay. Firstly, the cells were plated on 96-well cell plates and maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 24 h in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Each well was washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) after the medium was removed, and the IL-CDs of different concentrations were added to each well. After 24 h, each well was filled with 100 mL of MTT dye. After 4 h of incubation, 100 μL DMSO was added to each well and the optical density was measured using a plate reader.
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
s a m p l e s - A b s o r b a n c e
o f
b l a n k
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p - A b s o r b a n c e
o f
b l a n k
× 100 %

In a 37 °C, 5 % CO2 incubator, Hela cells were plated in 6-well cell culture plates and maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 24 h. To eliminate extracellular IL-CDs, the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4) after the medium was removed. Finally, images of the cells were obtained on a fluorescent microscope.

The IL-CDs were synthesized by utilizing [OMIM]BF4 as a precursor in a one-step microwave procedure. To improve their optical performance, the effects of type and amount of ionic liquid, microwave power, and microwave irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity were systematically researched. The flaws of CDs were modified because the long cationic carbon chains functioned like polymers, causing the fluorescence intensity to grow as the imidazolium cation alkyl chain of ionic liquids increased (Fig. S1A) (Guo, Wang, Zhao, Shu, & Wang, 2021; Zheng, Guo, Guan, Chen, Shu, & Wang, 2019). Therefore, [OMIM]BF4 was selected as the carbon source for further experiments. Furthermore, as the ionic liquid amount, microwave power, and microwave irradiation time increased, the fluorescence intensity increased at first, then decreased (Fig. S1B, C, D). According to the above research results, the optimal synthesis conditions were [OMIM]BF4, 2 g; microwave power, 490 w; microwave irradiation time, 80 s.

The size and morphology of IL-CDs were characterized by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A and 1B, the IL-CDs were homogeneously distributed spherical particles, and the average particle size of 2.40 nm with a standard deviation of ± 0.63 nm was obtained by performing calculations on 100 nanoparticles. The lattice fringes with an average spacing of 0.28 nm were visible in the high-resolution TEM image. The XRD pattern demonstrated a wide diffraction peak around 2θ = 20.4° (Fig. 1C), corresponding to the (002) graphitic in-plane lattice (Amjadi, Hallaj, & Mayan, 2016). Raman spectroscopy based on the Raman scattering effect was used to obtain the molecular vibration and rotation information of the sample by analyzing the scattering spectra of different frequencies of incident spectra. As shown in Fig. S2, the Raman spectra of IL-CDs exhibited two typical signal bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 under the 532 nm laser, responding to the disordered D band and the graphite G band, respectively. The former belonged to the sp2 hybridization of carbon, and the later was produced by the plane stretching vibration mode of crystalline graphite carbon. Moreover, the broad peak at 1460 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of CH3 (Wei et al., 2020).

The FT-IR spectra confirmed the presence of the stretching vibrations of the OH (3434 cm−1), NH (3112 cm−1), and CH (2930 cm−1) bonds after the production of IL-CDs (Han et al., 2016). The stretching vibrations of CO and CC caused two absorption peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 (Liu, Chen, Wang, Na, Chen, & Chen, 2019; Zhou, Yang, & Zhang, 2013). Because of the formation of graphite shape, the absorption peak of the CO stretching vibration (1063 cm−1) was different from [OMIM]PF4 (Fig. 1D). The above results demonstrated that IL-CDs had several amino, carbonyl, and oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces, indicating that they have promising applications in analytical detection and biological fields. In addition, because of the existence of amino groups, the zeta potential value was 17.9 mV, which made them more accessible to the cells (Fig. S3) (Wei et al., 2020).

The electronic structure and surface elemental composition were studied using the X-ray photoelectron spectrogram (XPS). There were five peaks at the binding energies of 284.7 eV, 401.7 eV, 532.4 eV, 685.4 eV, and 193.8 eV (Fig. S4A), corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, F 1 s, and B 1 s, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum was differentiated into four peaks at 284.4 eV, 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 286.3 eV, which could be assigned to C C/CC, C N, CO, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. S4B) (Liao et al., 2015). The presence of two nitrogen states, CN (401.0 eV) and NH (401.6 eV), was verified by the N 1 s spectra (Fig. S4C) (Ma et al., 2020). And two different peaks were divided at 532.1 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to CO and CO bonds (Fig. S4D) (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020). Furthermore, the peaks in the F 1 s spectra at 685.4 eV and 685.6 eV were ascribed to the characteristic peaks of the semi-ionic and covalently bonded C-F (Fig. S4E), respectively. At the same time, there were two peaks at 193.8 eV and 193.9 eV, attributed to B-C and B-O bonds (Fig. S4F) (Huang et al., 2020). The XPS spectra analysis further confirmed the results from the FT-IR spectra. It could be inferred from the above analysis that N, F, and B have been successfully doped into IL-CDs to enhance the photoluminescence properties of IL-CDs owing to the introduction of emissive energy traps to promote the emissive recombination of localized electron hole pairs (Du & Guo, 2016). Due to the influence of heteroatom doping, these CDs will exhibit excellent luminescence properties.

To explore the optical characteristics, the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured. Fig. S5A illustrated the presence of the π-π* transition of the CC (221 nm) structure and the n-π* transition of the CO structure (263 nm) (Xu, Yang, Li, Zhao, & Liao, 2015). These IL-CDs possessed a significant characteristic blue fluorescence under UV lamp (Inset) with the maximum emission at 435 nm and the maximal excitation wavelength remaining at 360 nm, and exhibited excitation-dependent emission (Fig. S5B). The varied sizes and surface functional groups led to different surface states, which explained why excitation dependent emission happened (Cai et al., 2018). In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 14.87 % was estimated using quinine bisulfate as a standard.

Stability is crucial for evaluating CDs, which can ensure the accuracy and reliability of CDs in practical applications. Fig. S5C illustrated that the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable at pH 3.0–11.0. However, under strong acidic and strong alkaline conditions, protonation/deprotonation processes can cause fluorescence intensity to significantly decrease (Ehtesabi, Hallaji, Najafi Nobar, & Bagheri, 2020). In addition, the IL-CDs still maintained a high fluorescence intensity when the NaCl concentration reached 1.0 mol/L (Fig. S5D). Moreover, no precipitation was observed in the solution of IL-CDs after 30 d (Fig. S6). Moreover, the stability of IL-CDs was tested at room temperature (25℃) and in a low-temperature chiller (4℃). After 20 d and 40 d of storage, an initial response decrease of more than 10 % was observed, indicating that the IL-CDs were stable for at least 20 d at room temperature. The above results demonstrated that the IL-CDs had good fluorescence stability, which was particularly helpful for the application of sensing and biological imaging.

The linearity between different concentrations of VP solution (0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100 μmol/L) and the fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity dropped as the VP concentration increased (Fig. 2 A), and the regression equation was obtained in the range of 0–100.0 μmol/L as y = -148.62ln(x) + 745.27 (DFε = 20, DFlof = 8, F = 2.45 less than 2.50). The proposed equation was observed to produce good fits to the measured data by the F-test (Table S1).

Since the actual samples contain many different interfering substances, we researched the selectivity of IL-CDs to VP in different ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, and F-), biological molecules (Cys, His, Glu, and Arg), pesticides (Diu and Mon), and similar compounds (Kae, Que, Til, Del, Cya, VC, and VE) (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, different concentrations (100, 200, and 300 μmol/L) of interfering substances and their effects on fluorescence intensity changes were investigated. The interfering signal was found less than 3.0 %, which was calculated by subtracting the fluorescence intensity change of the interfering substance added-sample with that of the original sample, and dividing the fluorescence intensity change of original sample (Fig. S7). Therefore, the synthesized IL-CDs were sensitive enough to detect VP even in the existence of interfering substances, which made the quantitative detection of VP possible. Furthermore, the LOD was reckoned to be 60.0 nmol/L by the mean value of 20 blank samples plus three times the mean standard deviation (Dong et al., 2019), which was lower than other reports for VP determination (Table S2). Therefore, the high selectivity and sensitivity endow IL-CDs with valuable application prospects in food analysis. In addition, the reaction between IL-CDs and VP could be completed rapidly within 1 min (Fig. S8).

To verify the feasibility of this approach, samples of tangerine, orange, lemon, grapefruit, and grapes were selected. Since some small molecules in the sample may affect the fluorescence signal, the standard curve method was compared with the working curve method. The t-test was applied and the statistical analysis showed no significant difference between these two methods with the 95 % confidence level. The results indicated that there was no matrix effect, thus the standard curve method was chosen for the subsequent quantitative determination.

The precision and accuracy of the present method were evaluated using three concentrations (1.0, 10.0, and 20.0 μmol/L) and three replicates of each concentration. The initial concentration of VP was obtained by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and this method (IL-CDs), respectively. As illustrated in Table 1 , the VP recoveries ranged from 97 % to 119 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 0.32 % to 2.43 %. The linearity of the relationship between estimated and actual concentrations was evaluated, and the slope was close to 1.0 (Fig. S9). In addition, the t-test was used to compare this method with the standard method (HPLC), and no significant difference was found between the two methods. The above results demonstrated the good analytical performance of IL-CDs, indicating this approach was feasible to assess VP in food samples.

Since the matrix, synthesis time, linear range, and detection limit can better reflect the analytical performance of the sensor, IL-CDs were compared with other VP sensors in these aspects (Table 2 ). The synthesized IL-CDs exhibited better detection limits and a larger detection range compared to the reported literature. The present method for synthesizing IL-CDs by microwave irradiation greatly shortened the synthesis time and made the experimental operation simpler. More importantly, the present method can achieve the determination of VP in complex matrices.

Compared with the single IL-CDs spectra, no new absorption peak was formed after adding VP, which indicated that there was no new chemical bond between VP and IL-CDs (Fig. 3 A, 3B). Thus, the mechanism of quenching was not a static process. The fluorescence lifetime spectra were approximately consistent in the absence and presence of VP, which suggested the possibility of FRET and dynamic quenching mechanisms was excluded (Fig. 3C). In addition, the UV absorption of VP at 300–400 nm overlapped considerably with the excitation spectrum of IL-CDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism might be IFE (Fig. 3D). The Parker equation was calculated to confirm the existence of IFE:
(1)
F
cor
F

2.3 d A

10

(2)
E
obsd

F

F
obsd , 0
(3)
E
cor
= 1 -
F
cor
F
cor , 0

Here, Fobsd and Fcor are the measured and corrected fluorescence intensities, respectively. Eobsd and Ecor are the efficiencies of the measured and corrected fluorescence intensity changes, respectively. Aem and Aex represent the absorbance at the maximum emission wavelength (435 nm) and the maximum excitation wavelength (360 nm), respectively. “d”, “s”, and “g” are the width of the cuvette, the width of the excitation beam, and the distance between the edge of the cuvette and the edge of the excitation beam, respectively. Table S2 illustrated that when the concentration of VP increased, Fcor/Fobsd and Eobsd-Ecor values increased, confirming the presence of IFE.

The cytotoxicity of IL-CDs was investigated thoroughly by using the MTT test. As Fig. S10 illustrated, the cell viability of both cells (MCF-7 and Hela) remained above 80 % after 24 h of incubation with 1.60 mg/mL of the IL-CDs. Therefore, the excellent biocompatibility of IL-CDs was demonstrated, which ensured the efficiency of bioimaging. In addition, the cell images further demonstrated the above conclusion. Hela cells were incubated with 0.2, 0.4, and 0.8 mg/mL IL-CDs for 24 h. Through fluorescence microscope observation, the cells emitted strong blue fluorescence and retained complete morphology (Fig. S11). The results indicated that the IL-CDs were expected to become excellent potential bioimaging agents.

In summary, IL-CDs were synthesized using [OMIM]PF4 as a precursor for the determination of VP in common fruits and cell imaging. The IL-CDs exhibited high fluorescence intensity, high quantum yield, and good stability under UV excitation, while producing high-resolution images in cell imaging experiments, which indicated the excellent optical properties of the synthesized CDs. In the present sensing system, the fluorescent signal of IL-CDs was efficiently quenched because the absorption spectrum of VP at 300–400 nm overlapped considerably with the excitation spectrum. After a systematic study, the IL-CDs as fluorescent probes of VP exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity. Additionally, the synthesized IL-CDs as biocompatibility imaging probes demonstrated broad application prospects in the field of biomedical analysis. In conclusion, this study provided a rapid and accurate sensing scheme for the detection of VP in complex matrices, which also promoted further biological applications of IL-CDs.

Chenzhao Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xiujuan Wang: Methodology, Validation. Yupu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Yihuan Tang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Yi Yang: Methodology, Validation. Bin Wang: Methodology, Validation. Shanshan Wei: Methodology, Validation. Zhibing Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft. Guoying Sun: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This wok was financially supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC), Science and Technology Research Fund for Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (No. 20190304011YY) and Department of Education of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20210762KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134898.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
